subject: ap-government
theme: The three branches of government share powers through overlapping checks and balances, producing ongoing institutional competition among Congress, the president, the bureaucracy, and the courts over policy authority.
core_concepts: Judicial review (Marbury v. Madison, 1803): Chief Justice Marshall established that courts may invalidate unconstitutional laws — making the judiciary a coequal check on Congress and the president despite judicial review not appearing in the Constitution's text.

Congressional powers: Article I grants the power to legislate, declare war, control spending, confirm appointments, and impeach — but internal rules (filibuster, cloture, committee gatekeeping, logrolling) often determine whether legislative power is exercised.

Presidential powers: Article II creates the executive — expressed powers include commander-in-chief, treaty-making, veto, and appointments; implied/inherent powers include executive orders, signing statements, and executive privilege (claimed in US v. Nixon, 1974).

The filibuster and cloture: Senate debate cannot end without 60 votes for cloture — this supermajority requirement gives minorities veto power over legislation and explains why much legislation fails despite majority support; gerrymandering shapes House composition.

Bureaucracy and iron triangles: federal agencies implement law through discretionary and rule-making authority; iron triangles (agency + congressional subcommittee + interest group) create durable policy coalitions; issue networks are looser, more fluid coalitions.

Federalist No. 70 (Hamilton) defends a single energetic executive — 'energy in the executive' requires decisiveness and accountability; Federalist No. 78 (Hamilton) defends judicial independence — life tenure insulates judges from political pressure and enables impartial review.

Congressional oversight: Congress monitors executive branch implementation through hearings, the power of the purse, confirmation powers, and the War Powers Resolution (1973) — which requires congressional notification within 48 hours of troop deployment and limits unauthorized military action to 60 days.

Baker v. Carr (1962) and Shaw v. Reno (1993): Baker held redistricting cases are justiciable (courts can hear them); Shaw held that racial gerrymandering violates equal protection — together they constrain how congressional districts are drawn.
exam_tip: Unit 2 is the heaviest AP Gov unit — expect stimulus questions using Federalist 70 or 78, congressional procedure scenarios, and iron triangle diagrams; know the specific powers of each branch and which require cooperation between branches (treaties require Senate ratification; vetoes can be overridden; appointments require confirmation).
